questions.question: What is a factor graph in the context of quantum error correction?

options.text: A graph showing physical qubit connections
A bipartite graph connecting variable nodes (errors) to factor nodes (stabilizers)

questions.explanation: A factor graph is a bipartite graph with two types of nodes: variable nodes (representing possible errors on each qubit) and factor nodes (representing stabilizer constraints). Edges connect variables to the factors they participate in.

questions.question: What do messages in belief propagation represent?

options.text: Beliefs about the probability of different error values

questions.explanation: Messages in BP represent beliefs about the probability distribution of error values. Variable-to-factor messages convey beliefs about the variable's error, while factor-to-variable messages convey constraints from the syndrome.

questions.question: Why does belief propagation struggle with surface codes?

options.text: The factor graph has many short loops
Surface codes have too many qubits

questions.explanation: BP is exact only on tree-structured graphs. Surface codes have highly loopy factor graphs with many short cycles, causing messages to cycle and potentially preventing convergence or giving suboptimal results.

questions.question: What is damping in belief propagation?

options.text: Mixing new messages with old messages to improve convergence

questions.explanation: Damping combines the newly computed message with the previous message: new = α × computed + (1-α) × old. This smooths updates and helps BP converge on loopy graphs where it might otherwise oscillate.

questions.question: For which type of quantum codes does BP work best?

options.text: LDPC codes with sparse parity checks and few short loops

questions.explanation: BP works best for LDPC (Low-Density Parity-Check) codes where the parity check matrix is sparse and the factor graph has fewer and longer loops. This allows BP to behave more like the ideal tree case.

questions.question: What is Ordered Statistics Decoding (OSD) used for with BP?

options.text: To post-process BP results and approach optimal performance

questions.explanation: OSD is a post-processing technique that uses BP's soft information to identify reliable bits, then exhaustively searches the remaining possibilities. BP + OSD can approach maximum likelihood performance.

questions.question: Belief propagation produces exact results on tree-structured factor graphs (graphs without loops).
questions.explanation: True. On tree-structured graphs (no loops), BP converges to the exact marginal probabilities. The algorithm is provably optimal in this case. Problems only arise when loops are present.
